name: Staphylococcus aureus
pres: 22-year-old college wrestler with painful fluctuant abscess on left thigh, low-grade fever.
id: Catalase+, coagulase+, golden colonies on blood agar, mannitol fermenter on MSA
id_q: Which test distinguishes S. aureus from S. epidermidis?
id_a: Coagulase test (S. aureus is coagulase-positive)
vir: Protein A (binds IgG Fc), coagulase, TSST-1, PVL (CA-MRSA skin), enterotoxins (food poisoning), exfoliative toxins
vir_q: Which virulence factor is most associated with CA-MRSA skin infections?
vir_a: Panton-Valentine leukocidin (PVL)
dis: Skin abscesses, bacteremia, endocarditis, osteomyelitis, pneumonia, toxic shock, food poisoning, scalded skin
tx: MRSA skin: I&D + TMP-SMX or doxy. Serious: vancomycin. MSSA: nafcillin. MRSA bacteremia: daptomycin if vanco fails
tx_a: Incision and drainage + oral TMP-SMX or doxycycline
res: MRSA: mecA gene → altered PBP2a. VRSA: vanA from enterococci. D-test for inducible clindamycin resistance
res_a: mecA gene encoding altered PBP2a with low β-lactam affinity
prev: Hand hygiene, contact precautions, mupirocin nasal + CHG baths for decolonization
prev_a: Intranasal mupirocin BID × 5 days + chlorhexidine body washes

name: Streptococcus pneumoniae
pres: 68-year-old with COPD, rust-colored sputum, fever, pleuritic chest pain. CXR: RLL consolidation.
id: Alpha-hemolytic, optochin-sensitive, bile soluble, Quellung reaction+
id_q: Which test distinguishes S. pneumoniae from other alpha-hemolytic strep?
id_a: Optochin sensitivity
vir: Polysaccharide capsule (>90 serotypes), IgA protease, pneumolysin, autolysin
vir_q: Most important virulence factor?
vir_a: Polysaccharide capsule (prevents phagocytosis)
dis: CAP (#1), bacterial meningitis (adults), otitis media (#1 in children), sinusitis. High risk: asplenia/sickle cell
tx: CAP outpatient: amoxicillin/doxycycline. Inpatient: ceftriaxone + azithromycin. Meningitis: ceftriaxone + vancomycin
tx_a: Amoxicillin high-dose or doxycycline
res: Altered PBPs (NOT β-lactamase). High-dose amoxicillin overcomes intermediate resistance. Macrolide resistance: ermB/mefA
res_a: Altered PBPs through stepwise mutations (NOT β-lactamase)
prev: PCV20 for adults ≥65. Childhood PCV series. Influenza vaccine. Smoking cessation
prev_a: PCV20 (Prevnar 20) — single dose

name: Group A Strep (S. pyogenes)
pres: 7-year-old with severe sore throat, fever 39.4°C, tonsillar exudates, anterior cervical LAD. No cough.
id: Beta-hemolytic, bacitracin-sensitive, PYR+. RADT and throat culture
id_a: Bacitracin sensitivity
vir: M protein (anti-phagocytic, molecular mimicry → rheumatic fever), streptolysin O, SPE (scarlet fever/STSS)
vir_q: Which factor causes rheumatic fever?
vir_a: M protein (mimics cardiac myosin)
dis: Pharyngitis, scarlet fever, impetigo, cellulitis, necrotizing fasciitis, STSS. Post: rheumatic fever, PSGN
tx: Penicillin V or amoxicillin × 10 days. NO penicillin resistance ever documented
tx_a: Penicillin V or amoxicillin × 10 days
res: NO penicillin resistance EVER. Macrolide resistance 15-30%
res_a: GAS has NEVER developed penicillin resistance despite decades of use
prev: Treat pharyngitis within 9 days to prevent RF. Penicillin prophylaxis for RF recurrence
prev_q: Primary reason to treat GAS pharyngitis?
prev_a: Prevent acute rheumatic fever (if treated within 9 days)

name: Group B Strep (S. agalactiae)
pres: 28-year-old at 37 weeks in labor. GBS screen positive at 36 weeks.
id: Beta-hemolytic, CAMP test+ (arrow-shaped), hippurate+, bacitracin-resistant
id_a: CAMP test (arrow-shaped hemolysis with S. aureus)
vir: Polysaccharide capsule (type III most virulent), sialic acid, beta-hemolysin
vir_q: Which serotype causes most neonatal disease?
vir_a: Type III (most meningitis and late-onset disease)
dis: Neonatal sepsis/meningitis (early <7d, late 7-90d), chorioamnionitis, postpartum endometritis
tx: IAP: IV penicillin G q4h until delivery. Cefazolin for mild allergy. Vancomycin for severe allergy
tx_a: IV penicillin G every 4 hours until delivery
res: Universally penicillin-susceptible. Clindamycin resistance 15-30%
res_q: What to test before clindamycin use?
res_a: Clindamycin susceptibility (resistance 15-30%)
prev: Universal screening 36-37 weeks. IAP for positive screens, GBS bacteriuria, prior GBS infant
prev_a: 36-37 weeks gestation (vaginal-rectal culture)

name: Enterococcus
pres: 72-year-old in ICU with catheter, fever, cloudy urine.
id: Gamma-hemolytic, bile esculin+, 6.5% NaCl tolerant, PYR+. E. faecalis (80-90%), E. faecium (10-20%)
id_a: Bile esculin agar + 6.5% NaCl tolerance
vir: Intrinsic antibiotic resistance, biofilm formation, aggregation substance (plasmid transfer)
vir_q: Why are cephalosporins ineffective?
vir_a: Intrinsic low-affinity PBPs that cephalosporins cannot bind
dis: Catheter UTI, subacute endocarditis, intra-abdominal infections, bacteremia
tx: UTI: ampicillin. Endocarditis: ampicillin + gentamicin. VRE: linezolid or daptomycin
tx_a: Ampicillin + gentamicin for synergistic bactericidal activity
res: VRE: vanA (high-level, transferable → VRSA), vanB. Intrinsic cephalosporin resistance
res_a: vanA — high-level, transferable, can spread to S. aureus (VRSA)
prev: Contact precautions, antimicrobial stewardship, remove unnecessary catheters
prev_a: Prolonged broad-spectrum antibiotics, especially cephalosporins and vancomycin

name: Escherichia coli
pres: 25-year-old woman with dysuria, frequency, suprapubic pain for 2 days.
id: Lactose-fermenting (pink MacConkey), indole+, metallic green sheen on EMB
id_q: Which agar shows metallic green sheen?
id_a: EMB (eosin methylene blue) agar
vir: LT/ST toxins, Shiga-like toxin (EHEC O157:H7 → HUS), Type 1 pili (UTI), P pili (pyelonephritis), K1 capsule
vir_q: Which pathotype causes HUS?
vir_a: EHEC O157:H7 (Shiga-like toxin)
dis: UTI (#1 cause), neonatal meningitis (K1), traveler's diarrhea (ETEC), bloody diarrhea/HUS (EHEC)
tx: UTI: TMP-SMX or nitrofurantoin. NEVER antibiotics for EHEC (increases HUS risk)
tx_a: Antibiotics lyse bacteria releasing more Shiga toxin, increasing HUS risk
res: ESBL → carbapenems. CRE (KPC/NDM) → ceftazidime-avibactam
res_a: Carbapenems (meropenem, ertapenem)
prev: Hand hygiene, cook ground beef thoroughly, food/water sanitation
prev_a: Cook ground beef to 160°F/71°C, avoid unpasteurized products

name: Klebsiella pneumoniae
pres: 55-year-old alcoholic with hemoptysis, currant-jelly sputum. CXR: RUL consolidation, bulging fissure.
id: Lactose-fermenting, large mucoid capsule, mucoid colonies, indole-negative
id_a: Bulging fissure sign from dense lobar consolidation
vir: Thick polysaccharide capsule, siderophores, urease, hypermucoviscous strains (string test+) → liver abscess
vir_q: What gives the mucoid appearance?
vir_a: Thick polysaccharide capsule (positive string test)
dis: Aspiration pneumonia (alcoholics), UTI, hepatic abscess (K1/K2), nosocomial infections
tx: Susceptible: ceftriaxone. ESBL: carbapenems. KPC: ceftazidime-avibactam
tx_a: Ceftriaxone pending susceptibilities
res: ESBL (CTX-M/SHV/TEM). KPC (carbapenemase). NDM-1 (pan-resistant)
res_a: Klebsiella pneumoniae carbapenemase — hydrolyzes carbapenems
prev: Infection control, antimicrobial stewardship, ICU surveillance
prev_a: Contact precautions and hand hygiene

name: Pseudomonas aeruginosa
pres: 45-year-old with CF, worsening productive green sputum, declining FEV1 over 2 weeks.
id: Non-lactose fermenting, oxidase+, blue-green pyocyanin pigment, grape odor, grows at 42°C
id_a: Pyocyanin (blue-green)
vir: Pyocyanin (ROS), exotoxin A (EF-2 inhibitor), alginate biofilm (CF), elastase, type III secretion
vir_q: Unique CF virulence factor?
vir_a: Alginate biofilm (mucoid conversion)
dis: Nosocomial/VAP, CF, catheter UTI, burns, otitis externa, ecthyma gangrenosum, hot tub folliculitis
tx: Pip-tazo/cefepime/meropenem ± aminoglycoside. CF chronic: inhaled tobramycin
tx_a: Piperacillin-tazobactam + tobramycin
res: Intrinsic: AmpC, efflux pumps, narrow porins. Acquired: ESBL, MBL (NDM/VIM)
res_a: AmpC β-lactamase + efflux pumps + restricted outer membrane permeability
prev: Antimicrobial stewardship, remove catheters, water quality in hospitals
prev_a: Minimize ventilator duration, HOB elevation, oral CHG

name: Neisseria meningitidis & gonorrhoeae
pres: 19-year-old college student with sudden headache, neck stiffness, petechial rash → purpura.
id: Oxidase+, chocolate/Thayer-Martin agar. N. meningitidis: maltose+. N. gonorrhoeae: maltose-negative
id_a: Maltose fermentation (meningitidis+, gonorrhoeae-)
vir: N.men: capsule, endotoxin/LOS (DIC), IgA protease. N.gon: pili (antigenic variation), Opa proteins, NO capsule
vir_q: Cause of purpuric rash?
vir_a: Endotoxin (LOS) → DIC → microvascular thrombosis → purpura fulminans
dis: N.men: meningitis, meningococcemia, Waterhouse-Friderichsen. N.gon: urethritis, PID, disseminated GI
tx: Meningitis: ceftriaxone + vancomycin. Gonorrhea: ceftriaxone 500mg IM
tx_a: Ceftriaxone + vancomycin (add ampicillin if >50 for Listeria)
res: N.gon: FQ resistance >30%, emerging ceftriaxone resistance. N.men: generally susceptible
res_a: Widespread chromosomal resistance (>30% resistant globally)
prev: MenACWY/MenB vaccines. Chemoprophylaxis for contacts. Safe sex for gonorrhea
prev_a: Close contacts within 7 days — rifampin, cipro, or ceftriaxone

name: Haemophilus influenzae
pres: 2-year-old unvaccinated child with high fever, irritability, bulging fontanelle.
id: Requires factor X (hemin) + V (NAD), satellite phenomenon, type b PRP capsule
id_a: Factor X (hemin) and V (NAD) — satellite phenomenon
vir: Type b PRP capsule, IgA protease. Non-typeable lack capsule
vir_q: Hib vaccine target?
vir_a: PRP capsule polysaccharide conjugated to protein
dis: Meningitis, epiglottitis (thumb sign), otitis media, pneumonia. Non-typeable: COPD exacerbations
tx: Invasive: ceftriaxone. Non-invasive: amoxicillin-clavulanate (30% β-lactamase+)
tx_a: 30-40% produce β-lactamase; clavulanate inhibits it
res: β-lactamase production (30-40%). Not altered PBPs
res_a: β-lactamase production (unlike S. pneumo which uses altered PBPs)
prev: Hib conjugate vaccine — reduced invasive Hib >99%
prev_a: Reduced invasive Hib disease by >99%

name: Legionella pneumophila
pres: 58-year-old smoker in hotel with high fever, nonproductive cough, diarrhea, confusion, hyponatremia.
id: BCYE agar (iron + cysteine), silver stain, urinary antigen (serogroup 1 only)
id_a: BCYE (buffered charcoal yeast extract) with iron and cysteine
vir: Intracellular in macrophages (prevents phagolysosome fusion), type IV secretion (Dot/Icm), biofilm
vir_q: How does it survive in macrophages?
vir_a: Prevents phagolysosome fusion via type IV secretion system
dis: Legionnaires disease (atypical pneumonia + GI/CNS + hyponatremia), Pontiac fever (mild)
tx: Azithromycin or respiratory FQ. NOT β-lactams (intracellular)
tx_a: Intracellular pathogen — β-lactams cannot penetrate macrophages
res: Intrinsically β-lactam resistant. Susceptible to macrolides/FQs
res_a: High intracellular concentrations; β-lactams do not penetrate cells
prev: Water systems >60°C, UV disinfection, cooling tower maintenance
prev_a: Maintain water >60°C in hospital/hotel systems

name: Listeria monocytogenes
pres: 72-year-old immunosuppressed with fever, headache, AMS. CSF: monocyte-predominant pleocytosis.
id: Tumbling motility at room temp, beta-hemolytic, grows at 4°C, CAMP test: shovel-shaped
id_a: Tumbling motility at room temperature (actin rocket tails intracellularly)
vir: Listeriolysin O (escapes phagosome), ActA (actin rockets for cell-to-cell spread), internalins
vir_q: How does Listeria spread between cells?
vir_a: ActA polymerizes host actin into rocket tails propelling bacteria into adjacent cells
dis: Meningitis (neonates, elderly, immunocompromised, pregnant), granulomatosis infantisepticum
tx: IV ampicillin + gentamicin. NOT cephalosporins (intrinsically resistant)
tx_q: Why add ampicillin to empiric meningitis in patients >50?
tx_a: Listeria is intrinsically cephalosporin-resistant — only ampicillin covers it
res: Intrinsic cephalosporin resistance. Susceptible to ampicillin/TMP-SMX
res_a: Cephalosporin resistance
prev: Avoid unpasteurized dairy, deli meats, soft cheeses in pregnancy/immunosuppression
prev_a: Avoid unpasteurized dairy, deli meats, soft cheeses, refrigerated smoked seafood

name: Mycoplasma pneumoniae
pres: 17-year-old with 2 weeks dry cough, low-grade fever, headache. CXR worse than exam (walking pneumonia).
id: No cell wall → no Gram stain, β-lactam resistant. Fried egg colonies. PCR or cold agglutinins
id_a: No cell wall = no PBP target for β-lactams
vir: Attachment via P1 adhesin, community-acquired pathogen, induces autoimmune responses
vir_q: Why does it cause autoimmune hemolytic anemia?
vir_a: Induces cold agglutinins (IgM) that cross-react with red blood cell I antigen
dis: Walking pneumonia, Stevens-Johnson syndrome, cold agglutinin hemolytic anemia, bullous myringitis
tx: Azithromycin, doxycycline, or FQ. NOT β-lactams
tx_a: Azithromycin (or doxycycline)
res: Macrolide resistance increasing (23S rRNA mutations, up to 90% in Asia)
res_a: Point mutations in 23S rRNA reducing macrolide binding
prev: Droplet precautions. No vaccine available
prev_a: Droplet precautions and respiratory hygiene

name: Chlamydia trachomatis
pres: 22-year-old woman with mucopurulent cervical discharge, dysuria. NAAT positive.
id: Obligate intracellular. Elementary body (infectious) vs reticulate body (replicating). NAAT gold standard
id_q: What are the two developmental forms?
id_a: Elementary body (infectious, extracellular) and reticulate body (replicating, intracellular)
vir: Serovars A-C: trachoma. D-K: urethritis/cervicitis/PID. L1-L3: lymphogranuloma venereum
vir_q: Which serovars cause trachoma (leading infectious blindness)?
vir_a: Serovars A-C
dis: Urethritis, cervicitis, PID, reactive arthritis, neonatal conjunctivitis/pneumonia, trachoma, LGV
tx: Azithromycin 1g single dose or doxycycline 100mg BID × 7 days
tx_a: Doxycycline 100mg BID × 7 days (preferred) or azithromycin 1g single dose
res: Generally susceptible. Treatment failure rare
res_q: Is antibiotic resistance a major concern?
res_a: Resistance is rare; treatment failure is usually due to reinfection or co-infection
prev: Screen all sexually active women <25 yearly. Partner treatment. Safe sex
prev_a: All sexually active women under 25, annually

name: Treponema pallidum
pres: 28-year-old man with painless genital ulcer for 1 week. Bilateral inguinal LAD.
id: Darkfield microscopy. Cannot be cultured. RPR/VDRL (screening) + FTA-ABS/TP-PA (confirmatory)
id_a: Nontreponemal screen (RPR/VDRL) then confirm with treponemal test (FTA-ABS/TP-PA)
vir: Outer membrane proteins for attachment, immune evasion through antigenic variation, crosses placenta
vir_q: What makes syphilis a great imitator?
vir_a: Ability to affect virtually any organ system and its diverse clinical manifestations across stages
dis: Primary (painless chancre), secondary (rash including palms/soles, condylomata lata), latent, tertiary (gummas, aortitis, tabes dorsalis)
tx: Primary/secondary: benzathine penicillin 2.4M IM × 1. Late latent: weekly × 3. Neurosyphilis: IV penicillin × 14d
tx_a: IM benzathine penicillin G — no penicillin resistance ever documented
res: No penicillin resistance ever. Azithromycin resistance increasing
res_a: T. pallidum has never developed penicillin resistance
prev: Screen high-risk populations. Partner notification. RPR/VDRL for all pregnant women
prev_a: All pregnant women at first prenatal visit (RPR or VDRL)

name: Borrelia burgdorferi
pres: 35-year-old hiker in Connecticut with 10cm expanding erythema with central clearing on thigh.
id: Ixodes tick transmission. Clinical diagnosis (erythema migrans). Two-tier serology: ELISA → Western blot
id_a: Clinical diagnosis for EM; two-tier serology (ELISA then Western blot) for later stages
vir: OspA/OspC surface proteins, complement evasion, immune modulation
vir_q: Which outer surface protein was the vaccine target?
vir_a: OspA (LYMErix vaccine, withdrawn 2002 — new vaccines in development)
dis: Early: erythema migrans. Disseminated: facial palsy, heart block, meningitis. Late: arthritis, encephalopathy
tx: Early: doxycycline × 14-21d. CNS/cardiac: IV ceftriaxone × 14-28d
tx_a: Doxycycline 100mg BID × 14-21 days
res: Susceptible to doxy and ceftriaxone. No significant resistance
res_a: No clinically significant resistance; doxycycline and ceftriaxone remain effective
prev: DEET, permethrin, tick checks within 36h. Prophylaxis: single dose doxy 200mg for high-risk bite
prev_a: Single dose doxycycline 200mg within 72 hours of high-risk tick bite

name: Mycobacterium tuberculosis
pres: 42-year-old from India with 3 months cough, night sweats, weight loss, hemoptysis. CXR: RUL cavity.
id: Acid-fast (Ziehl-Neelsen). Slow growth (2-6 wk) on Löwenstein-Jensen. GeneXpert MTB/RIF (rapid + rifampin R)
id_a: GeneXpert MTB/RIF — detects TB DNA and rifampin resistance in 2 hours
vir: Cord factor (prevents phagolysosome fusion), sulfatides, mycolic acid cell wall (acid-fast, antibiotic barrier)
vir_q: Why is TB difficult to treat?
vir_a: Thick mycolic acid cell wall creates impermeability barrier to most antibiotics
dis: Pulmonary (upper lobes, cavitary), miliary, meningitis, Pott disease (spine), renal TB. Ghon complex
tx: RIPE × 2 months then RI × 4 months. Latent: INH × 9m or rifampin × 4m
tx_a: RIPE × 2 months (Rifampin, Isoniazid, Pyrazinamide, Ethambutol) then RI × 4 months
res: MDR-TB: INH + rifampin resistant. XDR-TB: MDR + FQ + injectable. Always test before treatment
res_a: Resistant to at least isoniazid AND rifampin
prev: BCG vaccine (variable efficacy), airborne precautions, LTBI treatment, DOT
prev_a: Treatment of latent TB infection (LTBI) to prevent reactivation

name: HIV/AIDS
pres: 28-year-old man with oral thrush, weight loss, LAD. CD4 180.
id: 4th-gen Ag/Ab combo (p24 + antibody) → confirmatory differentiation assay. Viral load for monitoring
id_a: 4th-generation HIV-1/2 Ag/Ab combination assay (detects p24 antigen + antibodies)
vir: gp120/gp41 (CD4 binding/fusion), reverse transcriptase, integrase, protease, high mutation rate
vir_q: Why is a cure so difficult?
vir_a: HIV integrates into host genome as provirus and establishes latent reservoirs that ART cannot reach
dis: Acute retroviral syndrome, asymptomatic phase, AIDS (CD4 <200): PCP, toxoplasma, CMV, MAC, Kaposi
tx: 2 NRTIs + integrase inhibitor (e.g., tenofovir/emtricitabine + dolutegravir). Start immediately
tx_a: Tenofovir/emtricitabine + dolutegravir (INSTI-based, high barrier to resistance)
res: NRTI: M184V (lamivudine). NNRTI: K103N (efavirenz). Integrase: less common with dolutegravir
res_a: High genetic barrier to resistance — requires multiple mutations for resistance development
prev: PrEP (tenofovir/emtricitabine), safe sex, needle exchange, U=U, PEP within 72h
prev_a: Undetectable = Untransmittable — sustained viral suppression eliminates sexual transmission

name: Candida species
pres: 65-year-old in ICU on broad-spectrum antibiotics with persistent fever. Blood cultures grow yeast.
id: Germ tube+ (C. albicans), Sabouraud agar, CHROMagar for speciation. C. auris: emerging threat
id_a: Germ tube test (positive in C. albicans)
vir: Morphologic switching (yeast↔hyphae), biofilm on devices, adhesins, secreted aspartyl proteases
vir_q: Why is biofilm formation clinically important?
vir_a: Biofilm on catheters/devices is resistant to antifungals — line removal is often necessary
dis: Thrush, vulvovaginal, esophageal candidiasis (AIDS), candidemia, hepatosplenic, endocarditis
tx: Mucosal: fluconazole. Candidemia: echinocandin (micafungin/caspofungin). Remove central lines
tx_a: Echinocandin (micafungin or caspofungin) + source control (remove lines)
res: C. glabrata: dose-dependent fluconazole. C. krusei: intrinsic fluconazole R. C. auris: multidrug resistant
res_q: Why is C. auris especially concerning?
res_a: Multidrug resistance (fluconazole, amphotericin, sometimes echinocandins) + environmental persistence
prev: Antifungal stewardship, remove catheters, C. auris infection control (contact precautions)
prev_a: Contact precautions + EPA-registered disinfectants (C. auris persists on surfaces)

name: Aspergillus species
pres: 52-year-old post-BMT with fever, pleuritic chest pain, hemoptysis. CT: nodule with halo sign.
id: Septate hyphae, 45° dichotomous branching. Galactomannan antigen. CT: halo sign → air crescent
id_q: Key imaging finding in early invasive aspergillosis?
id_a: Halo sign on CT (ground-glass surrounding nodule) — air crescent sign in recovery
vir: Galactomannan cell wall, conidial hydrophobins (immune evasion), gliotoxin (immunosuppressive), angioinvasion
vir_q: What is angioinvasion and why does it matter?
vir_a: Aspergillus invades blood vessels → thrombosis, hemorrhagic infarction, hematogenous dissemination
dis: Invasive pulmonary (neutropenic), aspergilloma (fungus ball), ABPA (asthma/CF)
tx: Invasive: voriconazole (first-line). Alt: isavuconazole, liposomal ampho B. ABPA: steroids + itraconazole
tx_a: Voriconazole
res: Azole resistance increasing (TR34/L98H from agricultural fungicides). A. terreus: ampho B resistant
res_a: Agricultural azole fungicide use selects TR34/L98H mutations in environmental Aspergillus
prev: Posaconazole/voriconazole prophylaxis in neutropenic patients. HEPA filtration in transplant units
prev_a: Posaconazole prophylaxis during prolonged neutropenia and transplant

name: Cryptococcus neoformans
pres: 38-year-old with AIDS (CD4 45), 2 weeks progressive headache and confusion.
id: India ink: halo. Latex agglutination for capsular antigen (highly sensitive). Mucicarmine: red capsule
id_a: Cryptococcal antigen (CrAg) latex agglutination — >95% sensitive in serum and CSF
vir: Polysaccharide capsule (anti-phagocytic), melanin (antioxidant), urease, phospholipase
vir_q: Most important virulence factor?
vir_a: Polysaccharide capsule — prevents phagocytosis and suppresses immune response
dis: Meningoencephalitis (AIDS-defining), pulmonary, disseminated. Source: pigeon droppings
tx: Induction: ampho B + flucytosine × 2wk. Consolidation: fluconazole 400mg × 8wk. Maintenance: fluconazole 200mg
tx_a: Amphotericin B + flucytosine × 2 weeks
res: Fluconazole resistance rare but increasing. Flucytosine R if monotherapy
res_a: Rapid resistance development when used as monotherapy — always combine with amphotericin
prev: ART for HIV. Fluconazole prophylaxis if CD4 <100 in high-prevalence areas
prev_a: ART to maintain CD4 >100 — best long-term prevention

name: Histoplasma capsulatum
pres: 35-year-old cave explorer in Ohio River Valley with fever, cough, hilar LAD 2 weeks after spelunking.
id: Dimorphic: mold at 25°C (tuberculate macroconidia), yeast at 37°C (small, intracellular in macrophages). Urine/serum antigen
id_a: Small intracellular yeast within macrophages (and tuberculate macroconidia at 25°C)
vir: Intracellular survival in macrophages, thermal dimorphism, capsular polysaccharides
vir_q: Where does Histoplasma survive and replicate?
vir_a: Inside macrophage phagolysosomes — survives by modulating phagolysosome pH
dis: Acute pulmonary (self-limited), chronic cavitary (COPD), disseminated (HIV — hepatosplenomegaly, pancytopenia, oral ulcers)
tx: Mild: observation. Moderate: itraconazole. Severe/disseminated: ampho B → itraconazole
tx_a: IV amphotericin B (induction) followed by oral itraconazole (maintenance)
res: Generally susceptible. Resistance rare
res_a: Resistance is rare; itraconazole remains effective for most cases
prev: Avoid bat/bird droppings. N95 in endemic areas. Itraconazole prophylaxis for AIDS in endemic areas
prev_a: Bat and bird droppings — caves, chicken coops, old buildings with roosting birds

name: Blastomyces dermatitidis
pres: 45-year-old hunter in Wisconsin with chronic cough, verrucous skin nodules, weight loss.
id: Dimorphic: mold at 25°C, broad-based budding yeast at 37°C (pathognomonic). Urine antigen (cross-reacts with Histo)
id_a: Broad-based budding yeast (single bud with wide attachment)
vir: BAD1 adhesin (binds CR3 on macrophages), thermal dimorphism, WI-1 antigen
vir_q: Key morphologic distinction from other dimorphic fungi?
vir_a: Broad-based budding (vs narrow-based in Crypto, vs endospores in Coccidio)
dis: Pulmonary, skin (verrucous/ulcerative — most common extrapulmonary), bone, GU. Great Lakes/Ohio-Mississippi Valley
tx: Mild-moderate: itraconazole. Severe: ampho B → itraconazole
tx_a: Itraconazole (fluconazole less effective)
res: Generally susceptible. Fluconazole less effective than itraconazole
res_a: Itraconazole (superior to fluconazole for Blastomyces)
prev: No specific prevention. Avoid disrupting soil in endemic areas
prev_a: Great Lakes region, Ohio and Mississippi River Valleys

name: Coccidioides immitis
pres: 30-year-old construction worker in Arizona with fever, cough, arthralgias (desert rheumatism), erythema nodosum.
id: Dimorphic: arthroconidia in soil (VERY infectious), spherules with endospores in tissue. Serology (IgM/IgG)
id_a: Spherules containing endospores
vir: Spherule-endospore cycle evades immune response, SOWgp (spherule outer wall glycoprotein)
vir_q: Why are lab workers at special risk?
vir_a: Arthroconidia in mold form are extremely infectious — only 10 spores can cause disease
dis: Valley fever (acute pulmonary), chronic cavitary, disseminated (skin, bone, meninges). SW US. Risk: Filipino/African descent
tx: Mild: observation. Moderate: fluconazole/itraconazole. Meningitis: fluconazole lifelong
tx_q: Duration of treatment for coccidioidal meningitis?
tx_a: Lifelong fluconazole (relapse rate very high if discontinued)
res: Some fluconazole resistance. Amphotericin effective
res_a: Requires lifelong antifungal therapy — meningitis relapse rate >75% if fluconazole stopped
prev: Dust control, no vaccine. Azole prophylaxis for transplant patients in endemic areas
prev_q: Who is at highest risk for disseminated disease?
prev_a: Filipino and African-American descent, immunocompromised, pregnant women (3rd trimester)

name: Pneumocystis jirovecii
pres: 32-year-old with AIDS (CD4 85), progressive dyspnea, dry cough, bilateral ground-glass on CT.
id: Cannot culture. GMS/DFA stain on induced sputum/BAL. Beta-D-glucan elevated. LDH elevated
id_q: Lab marker elevated in PCP but not bacterial pneumonia?
id_a: LDH (elevated in >90% of PCP cases; degree correlates with severity)
vir: Attaches to type I pneumocytes, disrupts surfactant, triggers immune-mediated lung damage
vir_q: Why does the immune response worsen PCP?
vir_a: Inflammatory response damages alveoli more than the organism itself — this is why steroids help in severe PCP
dis: PCP (subacute dyspnea, bilateral GGO, hypoxia). AIDS-defining at CD4 <200. Also: transplant, chronic steroids
tx: TMP-SMX (first-line TX and prophylaxis). Severe (PaO2 <70): add corticosteroids. Alt: atovaquone, pentamidine
tx_q: When to add steroids to PCP treatment?
tx_a: When PaO2 <70 mmHg or A-a gradient >35 — reduces mortality by decreasing inflammation
res: DHPS mutations (sulfonamide target). Clinical significance debated
res_q: Drug that is both treatment AND prophylaxis first-line?
res_a: TMP-SMX (Bactrim) — first-line for both treatment and prevention
prev: TMP-SMX prophylaxis when CD4 <200. Secondary prophylaxis until immune reconstitution
prev_q: When to start PCP prophylaxis in HIV?
prev_a: CD4 count <200 cells/μL (or CD4% <14%, or history of oropharyngeal candidiasis)

name: Mucor/Rhizopus (Mucormycosis)
pres: 62-year-old in DKA with facial pain, black eschar on palate, proptosis.
id: Non-septate (pauciseptate), wide-angle 90° branching (vs Aspergillus 45°). Tissue biopsy essential
id_q: How to distinguish from Aspergillus on histology?
id_a: Non-septate/pauciseptate with 90° branching (Aspergillus: septate, 45°)
vir: Angioinvasive → thrombosis/infarction/necrosis. Iron-dependent growth. Exploits acidosis/hyperglycemia
vir_q: Why does DKA predispose to mucormycosis?
vir_a: Acidosis releases free iron from transferrin; hyperglycemia impairs neutrophil function — both favor Mucor growth
dis: Rhinocerebral (DKA), pulmonary (neutropenic), cutaneous (trauma), GI. Black eschar = tissue necrosis
tx: Amphotericin B + surgical debridement + correct underlying condition. Azoles do NOT work (except posaconazole/isavuconazole)
tx_a: Mucorales are intrinsically resistant to voriconazole — use only amphotericin, posaconazole, or isavuconazole
res: Intrinsically resistant to voriconazole and fluconazole. Only ampho B, posaconazole, isavuconazole work
res_q: Limited antifungal options — what is available?
res_a: Amphotericin B (first-line), posaconazole, isavuconazole — plus aggressive surgery
prev: Control diabetes/DKA. Avoid deferoxamine (iron chelator increases risk). Posaconazole prophylaxis
prev_q: Paradoxical drug that increases mucormycosis risk?
prev_a: Deferoxamine (iron chelator) — Mucor uses it as a siderophore to acquire iron

name: Plasmodium species
pres: 28-year-old from Nigeria with cyclic fevers, rigors, splenomegaly. No prophylaxis.
id: Thick/thin blood smear (gold standard). RDT (HRP2 for P. falciparum). Banana gametocytes = P. falciparum. Schüffner dots = P. vivax/ovale
id_a: P. falciparum — pathognomonic finding on thin smear
vir: P. falciparum: PfEMP1 (cytoadherence → cerebral malaria), rosetting, knob formation on RBCs
vir_q: Why is P. falciparum the most lethal species?
vir_a: PfEMP1 causes infected RBCs to adhere to endothelium → cerebral malaria, organ failure, high parasitemia
dis: P. falciparum: severe/cerebral malaria. P. vivax/ovale: relapsing (hypnozoites). P. malariae: chronic, nephrotic
tx: Uncomplicated falciparum: ACT. Severe: IV artesunate. P. vivax/ovale: chloroquine + primaquine (check G6PD!)
tx_q: What must be checked before giving primaquine?
tx_a: G6PD status — primaquine causes hemolytic anemia in G6PD-deficient patients
res: Chloroquine R in P. falciparum (widespread). Artemisinin R emerging in SE Asia (kelch13 mutations)
res_a: Artemisinin resistance (kelch13 mutations) in Southeast Asia — threatens global malaria control
prev: Chemoprophylaxis (atovaquone-proguanil, doxy, mefloquine). Bed nets. DEET. RTS,S vaccine
prev_a: RTS,S/AS01 (Mosquirix) — recommended by WHO for children in endemic areas

name: Toxoplasma gondii
pres: 35-year-old with AIDS (CD4 60), headache, confusion, focal deficits. MRI: ring-enhancing lesions.
id: Serology (IgG/IgM). Ring-enhancing MRI lesions. Definitive host: cats. Tachyzoites vs bradyzoites (cysts)
id_q: Key MRI finding vs CNS lymphoma?
id_a: Multiple ring-enhancing lesions (toxo) vs single enhancing lesion (lymphoma) — empiric treatment trial differentiates
vir: Intracellular parasite, forms tissue cysts (bradyzoites) that persist lifelong, reactivation with immunosuppression
vir_q: Why does toxoplasmosis reactivate in AIDS?
vir_a: Dormant bradyzoite cysts reactivate when CD4 drops <100 and T-cell surveillance fails
dis: CNS toxoplasmosis (AIDS, CD4 <100), congenital (chorioretinitis, hydrocephalus, intracranial calcifications)
tx: Pyrimethamine + sulfadiazine + leucovorin × 6+ weeks. Alt: TMP-SMX
tx_a: Pyrimethamine inhibits dihydrofolate reductase → bone marrow suppression; leucovorin (folinic acid) rescues host cells
res: Generally susceptible. Prolonged treatment needed
res_a: Long treatment duration (6+ weeks) and need for secondary prophylaxis until immune reconstitution
prev: TMP-SMX when CD4 <100 (also covers PCP). Pregnant: avoid cat litter, undercooked meat
prev_q: Dual benefit of TMP-SMX prophylaxis at CD4 <100?
prev_a: Prevents both PCP and toxoplasmosis

name: Giardia lamblia
pres: 25-year-old backpacker with 10 days of foul-smelling watery diarrhea, bloating, flatulence. No blood.
id: Stool O&P: pear-shaped trophozoites with owl-face. Stool antigen test (more sensitive). Fecal-oral/water
id_a: Pear-shaped trophozoite with two nuclei resembling an owl face
vir: Variant-specific surface proteins (VSP — antigenic variation), ventral disc attachment to intestinal epithelium
vir_q: Why does Giardia NOT cause bloody diarrhea?
vir_a: Non-invasive — attaches to surface of intestinal epithelium but does not penetrate; causes malabsorption not tissue destruction
dis: Watery foul-smelling diarrhea, bloating, fat malabsorption, NO blood/WBCs. Can cause lactose intolerance. IgA deficiency risk
tx: Metronidazole or tinidazole (single dose, better tolerated). Alt: nitazoxanide
tx_a: Tinidazole single dose (or metronidazole × 5-7 days)
res: Metronidazole resistance 5-20%. Tinidazole may work for metro-resistant strains
res_a: Tinidazole, nitazoxanide, or combination therapy for refractory cases
prev: Water purification (boiling, filtration), hand hygiene in daycare, avoid untreated stream water
prev_a: Boil, filter, or chemically treat all stream/lake water before drinking

name: Hepatitis Viruses
pres: 35-year-old IVDU with jaundice, fatigue, RUQ pain, dark urine. ALT 1240, AST 890.
id: HepA: anti-HAV IgM. HepB: HBsAg/anti-HBc IgM/HBeAg. HepC: anti-HCV + RNA. HepD: only with B. HepE: anti-HEV
id_q: Which serologic marker indicates HBV infectivity?
id_a: HBeAg (indicates active viral replication and high infectivity)
vir: HBV: DNA virus (only hepatotropic DNA virus), cccDNA persistence. HCV: RNA virus, high mutation rate. HAV/HEV: RNA, fecal-oral
vir_q: Why is HBV so difficult to eradicate?
vir_a: cccDNA persists in hepatocyte nuclei even with treatment — serves as template for reactivation
dis: Acute hepatitis (all), chronic (B and C), cirrhosis and HCC (B and C), fulminant (B+D, E in pregnancy)
tx: HBV chronic: tenofovir or entecavir. HCV: DAAs (>95% cure). HAV/HEV: supportive
tx_q: HCV cure rate with modern DAAs?
tx_a: >95% sustained virologic response with direct-acting antivirals (sofosbuvir-based)
res: HBV: lamivudine R (YMDD mutation). HCV DAAs: very low resistance with modern regimens
res_q: Which HBV antiviral has highest resistance rate?
res_a: Lamivudine (YMDD mutation) — tenofovir and entecavir have much higher resistance barriers
prev: HAV/HBV vaccines. Universal infant HBV vaccination. HCV: harm reduction, no vaccine yet
prev_a: Universal infant HBV vaccination (>95% effective at preventing chronic infection)

name: Herpes Viruses
pres: 22-year-old woman with painful grouped vesicles on erythematous base on lip, preceded by tingling.
id: HSV: Tzanck smear (multinucleated giant cells), PCR (gold standard). VZV: clinical/PCR. EBV: monospot. CMV: PCR viral load
id_a: PCR (most sensitive and specific) — Tzanck smear is rapid but less sensitive
vir: HSV: latency in trigeminal/sacral ganglia. VZV: dorsal root ganglia. EBV: B-cell latency. CMV: monocyte/macrophage latency
vir_q: Where does HSV-1 establish latency?
vir_a: Trigeminal ganglion (explains orolabial reactivation)
dis: HSV-1: oral herpes, temporal lobe encephalitis. HSV-2: genital. VZV: chickenpox/shingles. EBV: mono, Burkitt, NPC. CMV: retinitis, congenital
tx: HSV/VZV: acyclovir/valacyclovir. CMV: ganciclovir/valganciclovir. EBV: supportive
tx_a: Requires viral thymidine kinase for initial phosphorylation — only activated in infected cells
res: Acyclovir R: thymidine kinase mutations → use foscarnet. Ganciclovir R: UL97 mutations → foscarnet
res_a: Foscarnet (does not require thymidine kinase activation) or cidofovir
prev: Shingrix vaccine (>50). Safe sex for HSV. CMV: valganciclovir prophylaxis in transplant
prev_a: Shingrix (recombinant) for all adults ≥50 — even if prior Zostavax or prior shingles

name: Influenza
pres: 72-year-old with sudden high fever, myalgias, headache, dry cough in January. Nursing home outbreak.
id: RT-PCR (gold standard). RIDT (moderate sensitivity). Type A: antigenic shift (pandemic) + drift. Type B: drift only
id_q: What distinguishes antigenic shift from drift?
id_a: Shift: major reassortment (new H or N subtype, pandemic). Drift: minor point mutations (seasonal epidemic)
vir: Hemagglutinin (attachment), neuraminidase (release), segmented genome (enables reassortment/shift)
vir_q: Why can only influenza A cause pandemics?
vir_a: Only type A has animal reservoirs (birds, swine) enabling reassortment of H/N subtypes (antigenic shift)
dis: ILI, viral pneumonia, secondary bacterial pneumonia (S. aureus, S. pneumo), myocarditis, Reye syndrome (aspirin + children)
tx: Oseltamivir within 48h. Baloxavir (single dose). Avoid aspirin in children
tx_a: Within 48 hours of symptom onset for maximum benefit
res: Antigenic drift (seasonal). Oseltamivir R: H275Y neuraminidase mutation
res_a: H275Y mutation in neuraminidase — use baloxavir or zanamivir as alternatives
prev: Annual influenza vaccine (all ≥6 months). High-dose/adjuvanted for ≥65. Chemoprophylaxis with oseltamivir
prev_a: Annual vaccination for everyone ≥6 months — high-dose or adjuvanted for ≥65

name: RSV
pres: 8-month-old in winter with rhinorrhea × 3 days, then wheezing, tachypnea, retractions.
id: PCR (gold standard), rapid antigen. Syncytial formation in culture. Winter seasonality
id_q: Why is it called respiratory syncytial virus?
id_a: Forms multinucleated syncytia (fused giant cells) in cell culture
vir: F protein (fusion — syncytia formation, target of monoclonal antibodies), G protein (attachment)
vir_q: Target of palivizumab/nirsevimab?
vir_a: F (fusion) protein — antibodies prevent viral cell-to-cell fusion and entry
dis: Bronchiolitis (#1 cause in infants), pneumonia, croup. Elderly/immunocompromised: LRTI
tx: Supportive: O2, hydration, suctioning. NO bronchodilators/steroids for bronchiolitis. Ribavirin rarely used
tx_q: Why no bronchodilators for RSV bronchiolitis?
tx_a: Studies show no consistent benefit — airway obstruction is from inflammation and mucus, not bronchospasm
res: N/A — antivirals rarely used. Ribavirin limited benefit
res_q: Why is supportive care the mainstay?
res_a: No effective antiviral; the disease is self-limited and immune-mediated. Good supportive care is sufficient
prev: Nirsevimab (long-acting mAb for all infants). Palivizumab for high-risk. Maternal RSV vaccine (Abrysvo)
prev_a: Nirsevimab (single injection) for ALL infants + maternal RSV vaccine (Abrysvo) during pregnancy

name: Bordetella pertussis
pres: 6-month-old with 2 weeks of paroxysmal coughing, inspiratory whoop, post-tussive emesis, cyanosis.
id: Bordet-Gengou or Regan-Lowe agar. PCR preferred. Marked lymphocytosis on CBC (pathognomonic)
id_a: Marked lymphocytosis (WBC >20,000 with >60% lymphocytes) — pertussis toxin inhibits chemokine receptor signaling, trapping lymphocytes in blood
vir: Pertussis toxin (ADP-ribosylates Gi → increased cAMP, lymphocytosis, histamine sensitization), filamentous hemagglutinin (attachment), tracheal cytotoxin, adenylate cyclase toxin
vir_q: Why does pertussis cause lymphocytosis?
vir_a: Pertussis toxin ADP-ribosylates Gi protein on lymphocytes, inhibiting chemokine signaling → lymphocytes cannot leave the bloodstream
dis: Pertussis: catarrhal (1-2wk, most contagious) → paroxysmal (2-8wk, whooping) → convalescent. Infants: apnea
tx: Azithromycin (first-line). TMP-SMX alternative. Treat early for best effect. Reduces transmission
tx_a: During catarrhal phase — may not shorten paroxysmal phase but reduces transmission
res: Generally macrolide-susceptible. Erythromycin resistance rare
res_a: Macrolide resistance is rare but has been reported; azithromycin remains effective
prev: DTaP (children), Tdap (adolescents, adults, EVERY pregnancy). Post-exposure azithromycin
prev_a: Tdap vaccine during EVERY pregnancy (27-36 weeks) to provide passive neonatal immunity

name: Salmonella & Shigella
pres: 4-year-old at daycare with bloody diarrhea, high fever, abdominal cramping × 2 days.
id: Non-lactose fermenting. Salmonella: H2S+ (black on TSI/HE), motile. Shigella: H2S-, non-motile. Very low Shigella infectious dose (10-100)
id_a: Salmonella is H2S-positive (black colonies); Shigella is H2S-negative
vir: Salmonella: type III secretion, Vi capsule (S. typhi). Shigella: Shiga toxin (S. dysenteriae), intracellular spread via actin rockets (like Listeria)
vir_q: Which Shigella species produces Shiga toxin and causes HUS?
vir_a: S. dysenteriae type 1 — the original Shiga toxin producer
dis: Salmonella: gastroenteritis, typhoid (S. typhi), bacteremia, osteomyelitis (sickle cell). Shigella: dysentery, HUS, reactive arthritis
tx: Salmonella GI: usually self-limited (abx prolong carriage). Typhoid: FQ/azithromycin. Shigella: azithromycin/FQ (treat due to high transmissibility)
tx_q: Why treat Shigella but not Salmonella gastroenteritis?
tx_a: Shigella has very low infectious dose (10 organisms) — treatment reduces transmission in daycare/institutional settings
res: FQ-resistant S. typhi (South Asia). MDR Shigella (ampicillin, TMP-SMX resistance increasing)
res_a: Fluoroquinolone-resistant S. typhi from South Asia and multidrug-resistant Shigella globally
prev: Food safety, hand hygiene, water sanitation. Typhoid vaccine for travelers
prev_a: Travelers to endemic areas (South/Southeast Asia, Africa) — Vi polysaccharide or live Ty21a

name: H. pylori & Campylobacter
pres: 45-year-old with epigastric burning that improves with eating (duodenal ulcer). H. pylori testing ordered.
id: H. pylori: urease+ (breath test, stool antigen, biopsy urease test). Campylobacter: curved/S-shaped, oxidase+, 42°C, Skirrow agar
id_a: Urea breath test or stool antigen (both >95% sensitive/specific)
vir: H. pylori: CagA (oncogenic), VacA (vacuolating toxin), urease (neutralizes gastric acid). Campylobacter: Guillain-Barré-associated
vir_q: H. pylori virulence factor linked to gastric cancer?
vir_a: CagA protein (injected via type IV secretion → oncogenic signaling)
dis: H. pylori: PUD, gastric adenocarcinoma, MALT lymphoma. Campylobacter: GI (#1 bacterial in US), Guillain-Barré, reactive arthritis
tx: H. pylori: triple (PPI + clarithromycin + amoxicillin × 14d) or bismuth quad. Campylobacter: azithromycin
tx_a: PPI + clarithromycin + amoxicillin × 14 days (or bismuth quadruple therapy)
res: H. pylori: clarithromycin R (30% US). Campylobacter: FQ R (50%+)
res_q: When to use bismuth quadruple therapy?
res_a: When clarithromycin resistance is suspected (>15% local rate) or prior macrolide exposure
prev: H. pylori: test and treat symptomatic. Campylobacter: food safety (proper poultry handling)
prev_q: Most common food source for Campylobacter?
prev_a: Undercooked poultry — #1 source; proper handling and cooking prevents transmission

name: Rickettsia species
pres: 8-year-old in NC with fever, headache, myalgias, petechial rash on wrists/ankles spreading centrally. Tick exposure.
id: Obligate intracellular. Serology (IFA) — often negative early. Clinical diagnosis + empiric treatment. Skin biopsy immunofluorescence
id_q: Why is early serology often negative?
id_a: Antibodies take 7-10 days to develop — treatment must NOT wait for serologic confirmation
vir: Infects endothelial cells → vasculitis, increased vascular permeability, edema, petechiae
vir_q: Target cell of Rickettsia?
vir_a: Vascular endothelial cells — vasculitis is the hallmark of rickettsial disease
dis: RMSF (R. rickettsii — tick, centripetal rash), epidemic typhus (R. prowazekii — louse), scrub typhus (chigger)
tx: Doxycycline for ALL ages including children. Do NOT wait for confirmation. Delay kills
tx_q: Can doxycycline be used in children for RMSF?
tx_a: YES — doxycycline is recommended for ALL ages; benefit far outweighs teeth-staining risk
res: Susceptible to doxycycline. Chloramphenicol alternative (inferior outcomes)
res_q: Why is empiric treatment so critical?
res_a: RMSF mortality is >20% if untreated, <5% if treated early. Delay of even 1-2 days dramatically increases mortality
prev: Tick avoidance (DEET, permethrin, tick checks). No RMSF vaccine
prev_a: Tick avoidance: DEET on skin, permethrin on clothing, thorough tick checks within 24 hours

name: Clostridium species
pres: 72-year-old on clindamycin day 10 with profuse watery diarrhea (10/day), abdominal pain, WBC 28,000.
id: C. diff: GDH screen + toxin EIA or NAAT. Pseudomembranes on colonoscopy. C. perfringens: double-zone hemolysis. C. botulinum: mouse bioassay
id_q: Best diagnostic approach for C. difficile?
id_a: Two-step: GDH screen (sensitive) → toxin EIA or NAAT (specific)
vir: C. diff: toxin A (enterotoxin) + toxin B (cytotoxin). C. perfringens: alpha toxin (lecithinase → gas gangrene). C. botulinum: botulinum toxin (blocks ACh release). C. tetani: tetanospasmin (blocks inhibitory NT)
vir_q: Which toxin blocks acetylcholine release?
vir_a: Botulinum toxin — blocks ACh release at NMJ → descending flaccid paralysis
dis: C. diff: colitis, pseudomembranes, toxic megacolon. C. perfringens: gas gangrene, food poisoning. C. tetani: tetanus. C. botulinum: botulism
tx: C. diff: fidaxomicin (first-line) or oral vancomycin. Severe: oral vanco + IV metronidazole. Recurrent: bezlotoxumab, FMT
tx_a: Fidaxomicin (preferred) or oral vancomycin — IV metronidazole alone is no longer first-line
res: Not traditional resistance — C. diff overgrows due to microbiome disruption. NAP1/BI/027: hypervirulent strain
res_q: Why does C. difficile cause disease after antibiotics?
res_a: Antibiotics disrupt normal colonic microbiome → loss of colonization resistance → C. diff spores germinate and overgrow
prev: Antimicrobial stewardship, contact precautions, hand washing with SOAP AND WATER (alcohol does NOT kill spores)
prev_q: Why must soap and water be used instead of alcohol gel?
prev_a: C. difficile forms spores that are resistant to alcohol-based hand sanitizers — only mechanical removal with soap works
